title: Enterprise AI Platform on Azure | End-to-End Azure MLOps Pipelines
paragraphs: End-to-End Azure MLOps Pipelines for Production ML Deployment

This project demonstrates an end-to-end MLOps workflow on Azure for automating machine learning model training, evaluation, registration, and deployment. Using taxi fare prediction as the sample business scenario, the solution shows how organizations can convert raw business data into a production-ready prediction service through Azure Machine Learning and Azure DevOps pipelines.

While the model predicts taxi fares, the real value of the project is the reusable MLOps platform behind it. The same architecture can be adapted for real-world use cases such as demand forecasting, price prediction, customer churn prediction, risk scoring, and operational decision automation.
Business-first view of the Azure MLOps platform
The sample model predicts taxi fare cost using trip details such as distance, pickup and drop-off location, trip time, passenger count, vendor, and trip metadata.
The broader platform automates data registration, preparation, training, evaluation, model registration, deployment, and endpoint testing through Azure DevOps pipelines.
The real value is the reusable MLOps platform pattern, which can be adapted across business domains beyond taxi fare prediction.
Why enterprises need a repeatable ML delivery pipeline

Many companies build machine learning models in notebooks, but struggle to move them into production. Models are often trained manually, deployed inconsistently, and difficult to monitor or update. This creates delays, quality issues, poor governance, and operational risk.
This project solves that problem by creating a repeatable ML delivery pipeline where data preparation, model training, validation, registration, deployment, and testing are automated.
Full Azure ML workflow delivered through CI/CD
Reusable architecture across multiple industries
Key outcomes for delivery teams and business stakeholders
How business data becomes a production prediction service

A business team provides historical data. The data science pipeline prepares the data, trains the model, evaluates quality, and registers the approved model. The DevOps pipeline then deploys the model as an endpoint that applications or business systems can call for predictions.

The architecture separates infrastructure, training, and deployment into independent Azure DevOps pipelines. Business data is registered in Azure ML, processed through a training pipeline, evaluated, versioned in the model registry, and then deployed through either real-time online endpoints or lower-cost batch inference depending on the business use case.
Technical deep-dive pages linked from the business-first overview
Infrastructure-as-code, Azure ML workspace setup, compute foundation, and Azure DevOps integration.
Open module 01
Dataset registration, data preparation, training, evaluation, and model registration workflow.
Open module 02
Online deployment, endpoint testing, and operational deployment patterns for production ML.
Open module 03
FinOps thinking built into training, deployment, and infrastructure choices

This project was designed with cloud cost control in mind. The architecture separates training, deployment, and infrastructure pipelines so compute resources are only used when required, while production endpoints can be managed based on real business demand.

The training compute cluster uses a scale-to-zero configuration, meaning no training compute cost is incurred when the pipeline is idle. This makes the solution suitable for experimentation, scheduled retraining, and cost-controlled proof-of-concept deployments.

For production inference, the project supports both online and batch deployment patterns. Online endpoints are suitable for real-time API predictions but have a continuous running cost. Batch endpoints are more cost-effective for scheduled or offline scoring workloads where real-time response is not required.

For low-volume or scheduled prediction workloads, batch inference can be used to reduce cost. For real-time business applications, the online endpoint can be enabled with a clear understanding of the monthly compute cost.
Core services, resource settings, and main cost drivers

The biggest cost is the online endpoint because the deployment uses STANDARD_D2AS_V4 with instance_count: 1. If this runs 24/7, estimate around $70/month for VM compute alone before storage, logs, registry, and workspace support services.

Training compute is cost-controlled because the cluster is configured as training_target_min_nodes: 0, training_target_max_nodes: 4, and training_target_sku: Standard_D2s_v3. That means the training cluster can scale down to zero when idle.

For a portfolio/demo project, keep the endpoint deleted when not testing. Then the monthly cost should stay around $5-$40. For a small production setup, a realistic range is often $200-$500/month depending on traffic, uptime, monitoring, and retraining frequency.
content: Enterprise AI Platform on Azure End-to-End Azure MLOps Pipelines for Production ML Deployment This project demonstrates an end-to-end MLOps workflow on Azure for automating machine learning model training, evaluation, registration, and deployment. Using taxi fare prediction as the sample business scenario, the solution shows how organizations can convert raw business data into a production-ready prediction service through Azure Machine Learning and Azure DevOps pipelines. While the model predicts taxi fares, the real value of the project is the reusable MLOps platform behind it. The same architecture can be adapted for real-world use cases such as demand forecasting, price prediction, customer churn prediction, risk scoring, and operational decision automation. Production Workflow The broader platform automates data registration, preparation, training, evaluation, model registration, deployment, and endpoint testing through Azure DevOps pipelines. Reusable Platform The real value is the reusable MLOps platform pattern, which can be adapted across business domains beyond taxi fare prediction. Business Problem Why enterprises need a repeatable ML delivery pipeline The Problem Many companies build machine learning models in notebooks, but struggle to move them into production. Models are often trained manually, deployed inconsistently, and difficult to monitor or update. This creates delays, quality issues, poor governance, and operational risk. The Solution This project solves that problem by creating a repeatable ML delivery pipeline where data preparation, model training, validation, registration, deployment, and testing are automated. What The Project Does Full Azure ML workflow delivered through CI/CD Infrastructure and Setup Creates Azure ML infrastructure Creates compute for model training Registers training data Prepares and splits the dataset Training and Governance Trains a Random Forest regression model Evaluates model performance Registers the model after evaluation Uses Azure DevOps pipelines for CI/CD automation Serving and Testing Deploys the model to an Azure ML online endpoint Supports batch endpoint configuration Tests the deployed endpoint Supports production-style ML delivery Business Use Cases Reusable architecture across multiple industries Cross-Industry Applications Transportation: fare prediction, ride ETA, demand forecasting, driver allocation Retail: sales forecasting, price recommendation, inventory demand prediction Finance: loan risk scoring, fraud detection, customer churn prediction Healthcare: appointment no-show prediction, patient risk scoring Manufacturing: equipment failure prediction, quality inspection prediction Customer Operations: support ticket prioritization, churn prediction, next-best action Business Value Key outcomes for delivery teams and business stakeholders Value Delivered Faster model deployment Lower manual engineering effort Repeatable training and deployment process Better governance through model registration Operational Advantages Easier rollback and versioning using registered models Clear separation of infrastructure, training, and deployment pipelines Support for both real-time and batch prediction patterns Cloud-native design using Azure Machine Learning Business Architecture Story How business data becomes a production prediction service Architecture Overview A business team provides historical data. The data science pipeline prepares the data, trains the model, evaluates quality, and registers the approved model. The DevOps pipeline then deploys the model as an endpoint that applications or business systems can call for predictions. The architecture separates infrastructure, training, and deployment into independent Azure DevOps pipelines. Business data is registered in Azure ML, processed through a training pipeline, evaluated, versioned in the model registry, and then deployed through either real-time online endpoints or lower-cost batch inference depending on the business use case. 1 Business Data 2 Data Preparation 3 Model Training 4 Model Evaluation 5 Model Registry 6 Online / Batch Deployment 7 Prediction Service 8 Business Application Business Data | v Azure DevOps Repository | +--> Infrastructure Pipeline | | | v | Azure Resource Group | | | v | Azure ML Workspace | | | v | Azure ML Compute Cluster | +--> Training Pipeline | v Register Data Asset | v Data Preparation | v Model Training | v Model Evaluation | v Azure ML Model Registry | v Deployment Pipeline | +--> Online Endpoint -> Real-Time Prediction API | +--> Batch Endpoint -> Offline Batch Predictions | v Business Applications / Reports | v Business Decisions | v Monitoring, Feedback & Retraining Detailed Project Modules Technical deep-dive pages linked from the business-first overview Platform Foundation (IaC - Azure ML) Infrastructure-as-code, Azure ML workspace setup, compute foundation, and Azure DevOps integration. Open module 01 ML Training Pipeline Dataset registration, data preparation, training, evaluation, and model registration workflow. Open module 02 Production Deployment & Ops Online deployment, endpoint testing, and operational deployment patterns for production ML. Open module 03 Cost-Aware Azure MLOps Architecture FinOps thinking built into training, deployment, and infrastructure choices Cost-Aware Design This project was designed with cloud cost control in mind. The architecture separates training, deployment, and infrastructure pipelines so compute resources are only used when required, while production endpoints can be managed based on real business demand. The training compute cluster uses a scale-to-zero configuration, meaning no training compute cost is incurred when the pipeline is idle. This makes the solution suitable for experimentation, scheduled retraining, and cost-controlled proof-of-concept deployments. For production inference, the project supports both online and batch deployment patterns. Online endpoints are suitable for real-time API predictions but have a continuous running cost. Batch endpoints are more cost-effective for scheduled or offline scoring workloads where real-time response is not required. Cost Considerations Implemented Azure ML training compute configured with minimum nodes set to zero Training cluster scales up only during pipeline execution Dedicated online endpoint used only when real-time prediction is required Batch endpoint structure included for lower-cost offline inference Separate infrastructure, training, and deployment pipelines for better cost visibility Workspace uses Blob Storage, Key Vault, Container Registry, and Application Insights Azure DevOps pipelines can run within free-tier limits for small project usage Estimated Cost Profile Environment Type Approximate Monthly Cost Demo / portfolio setup with endpoint deleted after testing $5-$40 Occasional training with workspace and storage retained $10-$50 One always-on online endpoint $100-$180 Small production-style setup $200-$500 Multi-environment setup such as dev and prod $300-$700+ For low-volume or scheduled prediction workloads, batch inference can be used to reduce cost. For real-time business applications, the online endpoint can be enabled with a clear understanding of the monthly compute cost. Resources the Code Uses Core services, resource settings, and main cost drivers Resource Inventory Azure Resource Group Azure ML Workspace in eastus Azure ML Compute Cluster: Standard_D2s_v3 Training cluster min nodes: 0 Training cluster max nodes: 4 Azure ML Online Endpoint Online deployment instance: STANDARD_D2AS_V4 Instance count: 1 Azure ML Model Registry Blob Storage, Key Vault, Container Registry, Application Insights Azure DevOps pipelines Main Cost Drivers The biggest cost is the online endpoint because the deployment uses STANDARD_D2AS_V4 with instance_count: 1 . If this runs 24/7, estimate around $70/month for VM compute alone before storage, logs, registry, and workspace support services. Training compute is cost-controlled because the cluster is configured as training_target_min_nodes: 0 , training_target_max_nodes: 4 , and training_target_sku: Standard_D2s_v3 . That means the training cluster can scale down to zero when idle. Estimated Monthly Cost Scenario Estimated Cost Infrastructure created, no training, no endpoint running $5-$25/month Occasional training only $10-$40/month Training + one always-on online endpoint $85-$130/month Training + endpoint + logs/storage growing $100-$180/month If scaled to 2 endpoint instances $160-$260/month Dev + prod environments $180-$350+/month For a portfolio/demo project, keep the endpoint deleted when not testing. Then the monthly cost should stay around $5-$40. For a small production setup, a realistic range is often $200-$500/month depending on traffic, uptime, monitoring, and retraining frequency.
